# Collaboration Patterns

This chapter introduces almost no new Git. Its commands are ones you
already have: branch, push, pull, merge, resolve a conflict (Chapter 7).
What is new is the *pattern*, the small set of habits that lets two,
three, or five people change the same repository without overwriting
each other or letting `main` rot. Most collaboration disasters are not
Git failures; they are pattern failures, and they are preventable.

The conflict demo here is real, generated between two clones of a shared
repository pushing to the same remote (a local bare repo standing in for
GitHub, as in Chapter 9). Two researchers, Alex and Bo, both edit the
project's README. Their hashes and the abbreviated paths are real
output; only `~` is substituted for the home directory.

## Solo is still collaboration {.tier-beginner}

Working alone is collaborating with your future self and your other
machine. The same discipline pays off: a short-lived branch per task, a
quick self-review of the diff before merging, and a `main` you keep in a
working state so that the version you push (and a coauthor might later
clone) always runs. You do not need the full team apparatus, but the
core move (do work on a branch, bring it back deliberately) is identical,
and adopting it solo means nothing changes when a coauthor joins.

## A branching strategy you can actually keep {.tier-beginner}

The strategy that fits research, and that GitHub's own workflow
recommends, is deliberately minimal: keep `main` always working, and do
each unit of work (a robustness check, a referee response, a new figure)
on a **short-lived branch** off `main`. When it is ready, open a pull
request, review it, merge it, and delete the branch. The two rules that
keep this healthy are to branch *small* and to merge *often*. Long-lived
branches are where collaboration goes to die: a "v2-rewrite" branch that
lives for three months drifts so far from `main` that merging it back
becomes its own miserable project. Short branches that rejoin within
days barely conflict at all.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The single most common research-collaboration mistake is everyone
committing straight to `main` and pushing whenever. It works for a week,
then two people push overlapping changes, `main` breaks, and nobody is
sure which commit is to blame. The fix is not more care; it is the
pattern above plus a protected `main` (Chapter 11) so that changes
arrive only through reviewed pull requests. Make the safe path the only
path.
:::

## What actually happens when two people push {.tier-beginner}

Here is the everyday event that surprises beginners. Alex finishes
first, commits, and pushes to `main` with no trouble. Bo, who started
from the same point and committed separately, tries to push and is
**rejected**.

```bash
$ git push
To ~/ggremotes/teamproj2.git
 ! [rejected]        main -> main (fetch first)
error: failed to push some refs to '~/ggremotes/teamproj2.git'
hint: Updates were rejected because the remote contains work that you do
hint: not have locally. This is usually caused by another repository pushing
hint: to the same ref. You may want to first integrate the remote changes
hint: (e.g., 'git pull ...') before pushing again.
hint: See the 'Note about fast-forwards' in 'git push --help' for details.
```

This is not an error in the alarming sense; it is Git protecting Alex's
commit. A push only fast-forwards, so Git refuses to let Bo's push
discard work that is already on the remote. The message says exactly
what to do: integrate the remote changes first. Bo pulls.

```bash
$ git pull origin main
From ~/ggremotes/teamproj2
 * branch            main       -> FETCH_HEAD
   caa8bb8..e80fd33  main       -> origin/main
Auto-merging README.md
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in README.md
Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.
```

Because Alex and Bo edited the *same lines* of the README, the pull
cannot merge automatically, and Bo gets the conflict from Chapter 7,
now arriving through a pull rather than a local merge. The mechanics are
identical: Git marks the clash and `git status` shows `UU README.md`
(both sides modified).

```bash
$ git status --short
UU README.md
```

## Resolving a conflict together {.tier-beginner}

Bo opens the file, sees both versions between the markers, and edits it
into the single line the two of them actually want, deleting all the
marker lines. Then the resolved file is staged and committed, which
completes the merge.

```bash
$ git add README.md
$ git commit --no-edit
[main 40efd7a] Merge branch 'main' of ~/ggremotes/teamproj2
```

The graph now shows the two lines of work rejoining at the merge commit,
and the push finally succeeds.

```bash
$ git log --oneline --graph -3
*   40efd7a Merge branch 'main' of ~/ggremotes/teamproj2
|\
| * e80fd33 Refine headline wording (Alex)
* | 21bf289 Reword headline (Bo)
|/
$ git push origin main
To ~/ggremotes/teamproj2.git
   e80fd33..40efd7a  main -> main
```

The merge commit `40efd7a` ties the two lines of work together, both
contributors' commits (`e80fd33` and `21bf289`) are preserved underneath
it, and `main` once again has a single agreed README.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
A conflict in *prose* (which wording of a sentence) is resolved by
agreement and a re-read. A conflict in *analysis code* is more
dangerous, because both sides run. If Alex changed a regression to
cluster by county and Bo changed the same lines to cluster by state, the
markers show the textual clash but not which specification the paper
reports. Resolving it is a research decision the two coauthors must make
together, not a merge to rush through alone. Pick the spec on purpose,
then re-run it (Chapter 16 makes the same point about agents).
:::

## Reducing conflicts before they happen {.tier-advanced}

Conflicts are not eliminated by skill, but they are made rare by habit.
Pull (or fetch) before you start work and again before you push, so you
are always building on the current `main` rather than a stale copy.
Keep commits and branches small, so any overlap is a few lines, not a
whole file. And divide the work along the *file* grain where you can:
if Alex owns `02_analysis.py` this week and Bo owns the figures, they
almost never touch the same lines, and Git merges their work with no
conflict at all. Most low-conflict teams are not lucky; they are pulling
often and not standing on each other's files.

## Coauthoring a single commit {.tier-advanced}

Sometimes a change is genuinely joint: you pair-wrote a methods section
or debugged a script together, and one person commits for both. Git
records a single author per commit, but GitHub honours a
`Co-authored-by` trailer in the message, attributing the commit to
everyone listed.

```bash
$ git log -1
commit 98cbf1c641d94136e319827f03854680f6937e0d
Author: Bo Truong <bo@example.edu>
Date:   Fri Feb 13 11:00:00 2026 +0000

    Draft methods note with Alex

    Co-authored-by: Alex Kim <alex@example.edu>
```

The trailer is plain text at the end of the message (a blank line, then
`Co-authored-by: Name <email>`), and GitHub turns it into shared credit
on the commit. For research that contribution record is not vanity; it
is part of an honest authorship trail.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
On a team, `main` is the shared source of truth, and its value depends
entirely on it always being in a working, reproducible state. That is
the whole case for the pattern in this chapter: branch, review, and a
protected `main` (Chapter 11) mean every commit on `main` is one that
passed review and checks, so any coauthor can clone it and reproduce the
current results at any time. A `main` that anyone can break at any moment
is not a source of truth; it is a liability everyone has to work around.
:::

## What you can now do

You can run a project, solo or with coauthors, on a sustainable pattern:
short-lived branches off a protected `main`, pull requests for review,
and frequent small merges. You understand why a push gets rejected and
how to integrate and resolve the conflict that follows, you know to
treat a code conflict as a shared research decision rather than a merge
to rush, and you can give joint credit with a `Co-authored-by` trailer.
With the GitHub chapters behind you, Chapter 13 returns to advanced Git
mechanics for the research workflows (worktrees, submodules,
sparse-checkout) that the everyday loop does not cover, and Chapter 14
fits Git into the editors and tools you actually write in.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

Best done with a partner, or simulated with two clones of one repo:

1. Have two clones of the same GitHub repo. From each, edit the *same
   line* of the README differently, commit, and push. Confirm the first
   push succeeds and the second is rejected with "fetch first".
2. In the rejected clone, `git pull`, get the conflict, resolve it into
   one agreed line, commit, and push. Confirm both contributors' commits
   survive under the merge commit in `git log --graph`.
3. Repeat once but edit *different* files in the two clones. Confirm the
   second push still needs a pull, but this time merges with no conflict.
   Explain why.
4. Make a joint commit with a `Co-authored-by:` trailer and confirm it
   appears in `git log`. (On GitHub, check that the commit shows two
   contributors.)
5. Write two sentences on why a long-lived branch is riskier than five
   short ones, in terms of the conflicts you saw above.
